siliconalley:client_welcome: Heard you run a sharp IT shop. Keep delivering quality and we'll keep sending work your way.
siliconalley:client_greeting: Good to hear from you. Keep shipping quality and the contracts will keep coming.
siliconalley:notify_projectcomplete: {business} shipped {product} {version} — review {review}/10, quality {quality}, +{payout} (reputation x{repmult}, market x{marketmult})
siliconalley:notify_ready: {business}: {product} is ready to release — press F9 or click here
siliconalley:notify_devdone: {business}: {product} build is done — send it to QA or release it (F9)
siliconalley:notify_startproject: {business} is idle — start the next project (press F9 or click here)
siliconalley:notify_dealfail: {business} missed {publisher}'s deadline — no bonus, reputation lost
siliconalley:notify_dealwarn: {business}: {publisher} deadline in {days}d — ship before it lapses
siliconalley:client_status_none: No Silicon Alley studios running yet. Start a Software Engineering Studio, Cyber Security Firm, or Game Developer Studio in an office, then staff it with Programmers at Computer Workstations.
siliconalley:client_registration_failed: Silicon Alley loaded, but its business types did not register. Check Player.log for 'SiliconAlley: registration health' and verify the mod install includes AssetBundles/Windows/siliconalley.unity3d.
siliconalley:notify_helpnudge: New to Silicon Alley? Open the in-game guide.
siliconalley:dash_empty: No Silicon Alley studios running yet. Start one of the Silicon Alley business types in an office, then staff it with Programmers at Computer Workstations.
siliconalley:dash_registration_failed: Silicon Alley business types did not register. Check Player.log for the registration health line and verify the AssetBundles/Windows bundle is installed.
siliconalley:notify_design: {business}: set the concept for {product} — press F9 or click here
siliconalley:screen_test_bugs: Open issues (bugs): {bugs} · polish {polish}% — Testing burns bugs down (×2 toward quality)
siliconalley:screen_release_ready: Fully tested · market demand {demand} (release in a peak earns more)
siliconalley:screen_release_testing: In testing · market demand {demand} — release any moment (reviews worse early)
siliconalley:screen_update_ready: Update ready for your live catalog · market demand {demand}
siliconalley:screen_update_needstaff: Staff the studio to ship an update
siliconalley:screen_idle_summary: No active project — start the next version when you're ready.
siliconalley:screen_idle_status: Your staff are idle. Start a project to put them to work.
siliconalley:screen_dev_done: Build complete · market demand {demand} — send to QA or release
siliconalley:screen_dev_inprogress: In development — send to testing once the build is done, or release early
siliconalley:screen_mkt_synergy: Marketing synergy: +{rate}/h free from {count} agency(ies) you operate
siliconalley:screen_mkt_press_build: Press Build ({cost}) — big bump, best late in Development
siliconalley:screen_pub_none: No deal — sign a publisher to promote this release on a deadline (focus match pays more).
siliconalley:screen_editable: Set scope & focus, then lock the concept.
siliconalley:screen_locked: Concept locked — focus on the build.
siliconalley:screen_nostudio: No Silicon Alley studio. Start one of the Silicon Alley business types in an office, then press F9.
siliconalley:screen_registration_failed: Silicon Alley business types did not register. Check Player.log for 'SiliconAlley: registration health' and reinstall the mod if the asset bundle is missing.
siliconalley:wiz_components_header: Components — build in-house or license the stack
siliconalley:wiz_tools_header: Editors & tools — build in-house or license
siliconalley:wiz_tool_licensed: licensed from {vendor} · royalty {royalty}% · +{quality}% quality · tap to build ${cost}
siliconalley:wiz_market_header: Market — who is it for? (price vs volume)
siliconalley:wiz_alloc_hint: Select 2+ features in the Design step to allocate and target the market.
siliconalley:wiz_targeting_fit: Market fit: revenue {market} · quality {quality} (vs even split)
siliconalley:wiz_deps_header: Dependencies — does each feature have a tool?
siliconalley:wiz_deps_readout: Coverage {covered}/{total} · uncovered features cap quality at {ceiling}
siliconalley:client_contract_offer: A client has a fixed-scope job for {studio}. Deliver within {days} days for {payout}. Staff the studio to work it — it pauses your own product until it's done.
siliconalley:client_contract_accepted: Deal. {studio} takes the contract — deliver within {days} days for {payout}. Keep it staffed to get it done on time.
siliconalley:screen_contract: {progress} done · due in {days}d · pays {payout} (pauses your product)
siliconalley:notify_contractfail: {business} missed a contract deadline — reputation dropped
help_siliconalley-overview_content:
**Silicon Alley** adds three IT companies to the game. Found one in an **Office** building and staff it with **Programmers** hired from **City Workforce Inc.**, working at **Computer Workstations**.

Each studio designs software projects that ship for a quality-scored payout, then earn recurring support income from their installed base — scaled by reputation and neighbourhood competition.

**Business types**

- [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio)
- [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity)
- [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio)

**Goods & services**

- [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense)
- [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit)
- [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame)

**Guides**

- [Getting Started](siliconalley-getting-started) — found a studio and ship your first product
- [Design Wizard](siliconalley-wizard) — plan and design a project

**Economy & market**

- [Contracts](siliconalley-contracts)
- [Market Demand](siliconalley-market-demand)
- [Marketing](siliconalley-marketing)
- [Publisher Deals](siliconalley-publishers)
- [Product Lifecycle](siliconalley-lifecycle)
- [Bugs & Reviews](siliconalley-bugs-reviews)
help_siliconalley-softwarestudio_content:
**Software Engineering Studio** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team designs and ships business software as a digital product, so customers are reached through releases rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)
* [Graphic Designer](skill-graphicdesigner)

**How the studio works**

The studio works on one project at a time. Open the design window with **F9** to plan and steer it, and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). The project moves through four stages that you push forward: **Design** (Graphic Designers lead the concept), **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, but the later you ship the higher the quality. A release ships a [Software License](siliconalley-softwarelicense), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your installed base, and returns the studio to idle for the next project.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Cloud Sync, Plugin API, Enterprise SSO…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms** for wider reach, choose an **audience segment** (a price-versus-volume trade-off), and resolve **dependencies**. Putting more effort into the features the market wants most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, your **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). After launch the installed base pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals** for a deadline bonus, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview) for the bigger picture.
help_siliconalley-cybersecurity_content:
**Cyber Security Firm** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team delivers security work — sold as audits — as a digital service, so clients are handled through engagements rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)

**How the studio works**

The firm works on one engagement at a time. Open the design window with **F9** and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). Unlike the other studios this one has no separate design discipline — your programmers plan the concept themselves. The project moves through four stages you push forward: **Design**, **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, with later ships scoring higher quality. A release ships a [Security Audit](siliconalley-securityaudit), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your installed base, and returns the firm to idle.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Threat Intel Feed, Compliance Reporting, Zero-Trust Module…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms**, choose an **audience segment** (price versus volume), and resolve **dependencies**. Allocating effort to the features the market wants most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). The installed base then pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals**, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-gamestudio_content:
**Game Developer Studio** businesses operate out of office buildings.

Your team builds and ships video games as a digital product, reached through releases rather than walk-in foot traffic.

The business requires the following furniture to function:

* [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

Businesses of this type can sell:

* [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame)

Employees with the following skills can be assigned:

* [Programmer](skill-programmer)
* [Graphic Designer](skill-graphicdesigner)

**How the studio works**

The studio works on one game at a time. Open the design window with **F9** and staff it with Programmers hired from City Workforce Inc. seated at a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation). The project moves through four stages you push forward: **Design** (Graphic Designers lead the concept), **Development** (*Start development* confirms the design), **Testing** (*Send to testing* to burn down bugs), and **Release** — ship whenever you like, with later ships scoring higher quality. A release ships a [Video Game](siliconalley-videogame), pays a one-off launch sum, grows your player base, and returns the studio to idle.

**Designing a project**

The design wizard turns choices into quality and reach: pick **features** (Advanced Graphics, Multiplayer Netcode, Mod Support…) to raise the quality ceiling, **build or license tools**, target **platforms** (PC, Console, Mobile, Web) for wider reach, choose an **audience segment** (price versus volume), and resolve **dependencies**. Putting effort into the features players want most lifts both quality and sales.

**Earning**

Launch pay scales with **quality**, **reputation**, the **market-demand** cycle and neighbourhood **competition**, and is dented by residual **bugs** (which also lower the 0–10 **review** score). Your player base then pays recurring **support income**; ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep it fresh, sign **publisher deals**, or take fixed-scope **contracts** from the phone. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-softwarelicense_conten:
**Software License** is the product shipped by the [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team designs and develops it as a project; when you **release** it, it sells for a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your installed base.

After launch the product keeps earning recurring **support income** from everyone who owns it, and you can ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-securityaudit_content:
**Security Audit** is the service shipped by the [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team scopes and delivers it as a project; when you **release** it, it pays a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your installed base of clients.

After launch it keeps earning recurring **support income**, and you can ship **updates** and follow-up **engagements** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-videogame_content:
**Video Game** is the product shipped by the [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) — a Silicon Alley business you run from an office.

Unlike a physical good it isn't stocked on a shelf or built from a Factory recipe. Your team designs and develops it as a project; when you **release** it, it sells for a one-off, quality-scored launch sum and grows your player base.

After launch it keeps earning recurring **support income** from its players, and you can ship **updates** and **sequels** to keep that revenue alive. Launch pay and support both scale with quality, reputation, market demand and competition.

See the [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) page for the full project loop, or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-wizard_content:
**Design Wizard**

Every Silicon Alley project starts in the design wizard — the step-by-step planner inside your studio's design window. Open it with **F9** (or from a project notification), then click **Start project** to enter the **Design** stage and the wizard appears. Move with **Back** and **Next**; on the final step **Start development** locks your plan and your programmers begin building it.

The wizard has five steps — **Concept**, **Dependencies**, **Components**, **Market** and **Summary**. Your choices feed two things: the project's **size** (how long it takes to build) and its **quality ceiling** (the score its quality can climb toward during Development and Testing).

**Concept**

* **Scope** — *Quick win*, *Standard* or *Ambitious*. Quick is half the work for lower pay but barely minds rivals; Standard is the baseline; Ambitious is twice the work for premium pay, but a crowded neighbourhood bites harder. Scope locks when development begins, so changing it only affects your next project.
* **Product name** — name the release (leave blank for a default); it's recorded in the studio's release history.
* **Design focus** — a Polish ↔ Speed slider that biases the Design phase.

**Dependencies — features & tools**

* **Features** — pick from five per studio (e.g. Cloud Sync, Multiplayer Netcode, Zero-Trust Module). Each raises the **quality ceiling** but also adds **size** (more dev time). None are mandatory — more features mean a better but bigger product.
* **Editors & tools** — each tool cycles **Off → License → Build & Own**. *Build* costs one-off R&D cash, then it's a free, reusable studio asset forever. *License* costs nothing up front but takes an ongoing **royalty** from launch and support revenue. Either way using a tool raises the ceiling — only *licensed* tools charge royalty.
* **Coverage** — each feature you picked needs a covering tool. An **uncovered** feature lowers the quality ceiling (−12% each). Full coverage — or a project with no features — carries no penalty, so pick features you can back with tools.

**Components**

Build-or-buy the product's underlying stack (OS runtime, framework, backend…). Each slot cycles **Off → license a vendor → build in-house**. *Building* costs one-off R&D cash, adds a quality bonus, and becomes a reusable asset; *licensing a vendor* adds a quality bonus but takes an ongoing royalty.

**Market**

* **Operating systems** — toggle the platforms you ship on. More platforms **widen launch reach** (a bigger installed base) but add **porting** work (more size). With none selected you ship to a single home platform — reach is never zero. Reach grows your installed base, not the cash price.
* **Segment** — who it's for, as a price ↔ volume trade-off: *Broad* (neutral), *Enterprise* (high price, low volume), *Prosumer* (mid), *Consumer* (low price, high volume). Price scales the launch payout; volume scales the installed base.
* **Allocation & demand** — set how much to weight each selected feature, then check the demand panel: the market wants a **rotating mix** of product aspects that drifts over time. Matching what it wants — versus an even split — lifts **both** quality and launch revenue; a neutral split has no effect.

**Summary**

A read-only review before you commit: scope, size and ship estimate, the projected **quality ceiling**, **dependency coverage**, **up-front cost** (R&D for anything you build), **ongoing royalties** (from anything you license), and the **reachable market** (platform reach × segment volume, with price shown separately). Happy with it? **Start development** locks the design and coding begins.

**The quality ceiling**

Most choices feed one number — the quality ceiling your project climbs toward in Development and Testing. **Features, tools, built or licensed components, and a well-targeted feature allocation raise it; features left without a covering tool lower it.** Aim for features you can cover, a stack that fits your budget, and a market you've actually targeted.

See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview), or a studio page — [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio), [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity), [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) — for what each studio designs.
help_siliconalley-contracts_content:
**Contracts**

A contract is a fixed-scope side job a client offers by phone — guaranteed pay for a set amount of work, on a deadline.

**Getting one**

Call the **Silicon Alley Clients** contact on your phone. If one of your studios has no contract running, the client offers a gig and you can **Accept contract**. The offer names the studio, the deadline (14–30 days) and the flat fee.

**While it runs**

The studio's staff work the contract **instead of** their own product — your current project **pauses** (its progress is kept) until the contract is delivered. Keep the studio staffed so it lands on time. The project screen (**F9**) shows a **Contract job** bar with progress, days left and the payout.

**Payout & deadlines**

* **Deliver on time** — you're paid the agreed fee, scaled a little by the studio's quality.
* **Miss the deadline** — no pay, and the studio's reputation takes a small hit.

Contract fees are fixed when you accept — market demand doesn't change them. They're a steady way to earn between product launches. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-market-demand_content:
**Market Demand**

Each product category's demand rises and falls over time, so the same launch is worth more in a peak than in a trough.

**How it works**

* Demand swings about **±25%** around normal on a slow, roughly **72-day** cycle.
* The three categories — business software, security, games — peak at **different times**, so one studio can be booming while another waits.
* You can't set demand directly; it follows the in-game calendar.

**What it affects**

Demand scales your **launch payout**, recurring **support income**, and **update** revenue. It does **not** change fixed sums like contract fees or an agreed publisher bonus.

**Using it**

* Watch the demand trend on the studio dashboard (**F8**) — green **▲** when rising, amber **▼** when falling, with the current multiplier.
* The project screen (**F9**) shows the same multiplier near the Release button: **shipping (or releasing an update) in a peak earns more**.
* Within a category you can chase demand by weighting the [Design Wizard](siliconalley-wizard)'s feature allocation toward the aspects the market currently wants.

See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-marketing_content:
**Marketing**

Marketing builds **awareness** and **hype** before a launch — for a bigger, better-reviewed debut.

**Where**

Open the studio's project screen (**F9**). Once a project's design is locked (Development onward), a **Marketing** section shows your campaign options and the current Awareness / Hype.

**Campaigns**

* **Press Release** (~$1,500) — a quick awareness bump.
* **Press Build** (~$6,000) — a big awareness boost; worth most when fired **late in Development**.
* **Hype** (~$2,500) — adds hype, which **slows awareness decay** so it crests near launch.
* **Ad Spend** (toggle, ~$120/hour) — steady awareness while it's on; switches off if you can't afford it.

**What it does**

Awareness adds extra **installed base** at launch and can nudge the **review score** up by as much as a point; hype keeps that awareness from fading between phases.

**Agency synergy**

If you also run a base-game **Marketing Agency**, every agency you operate feeds your studios **free awareness each hour** — the Marketing section shows an **Agency synergy** row when one is active.

Pair this with [Publisher Deals](siliconalley-publishers); see also [Bugs & Reviews](siliconalley-bugs-reviews) and the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-publishers_content:
**Publisher Deals**

A publisher promotes one release for a **bonus** — if you ship before the deadline.

**Signing**

On the project screen (**F9**), once the design is locked, a **Publisher deal** section offers cards from the publishers open to you — a generalist plus the partner that matches your studio's field. Each card shows the **bonus**, the **deadline** and your **reputation** with that publisher. Click **Sign** to lock the deal.

**Publishers**

* **IndieLabel Collective** — works with any studio.
* **PixelForge Publishing** — games.
* **OfficeWorks Press** — business software.
* **Sentinel Security Media** — security.

A publisher whose **focus matches** your product pays more. Higher-tier publishers and a better relationship give **tighter deadlines but bigger pay**, and the bonus scales with your review score.

**Delivering**

* **Ship on time** — the bonus is paid on top of your normal launch revenue, and your reputation with that publisher grows, improving future deals.
* **Miss the deadline** — no bonus and your standing with that publisher drops (you're warned a couple of days out).

Pair a deal with [Marketing](siliconalley-marketing) for the strongest launch. See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-lifecycle_content:
**Product Lifecycle**

A shipped product keeps earning — and you keep it alive with **updates** and **sequels**.

**Support income**

Every product with an installed base pays passive **support income** each hour. The rate is tunable in the mod options — the **Support income** slider.

**Aging**

Support **fades** as a product gets stale — freshness drops from full toward a floor over about **60 days** since its last release. Shipping an update or a new version refreshes it.

**Updates**

A live product can take an update about **once a week**. When one's due, the project screen (**F9**) shows an **Updates** section with a **Release update** button (the studio must be staffed). An update pays a revenue bump and **resets freshness**, keeping the income flowing.

**Sequels**

Every release bumps the product's **version**. A **sequel (v2+)** launches bigger by leveraging the previous version's installed base and your **IP reputation** — but it's judged against a **higher review bar**. A strong sequel builds IP reputation; a weak one erodes it. After each ship the studio returns to idle, ready to start the next version.

See [Bugs & Reviews](siliconalley-bugs-reviews) and the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-bugs-reviews_content:
**Bugs & Reviews**

How buggy a product ships sets its **quality**, and quality drives its **review score**.

**Bugs**

* Bugs pile up during **Development** — and **twice as fast under Overtime**.
* **Testing** burns them down (QA work counts double toward a clean build).
* Bugs left at launch **cut the product's quality** — a very buggy build can lose up to half of it, dragging down payout, reputation and the review.

During Testing the project screen (**F9**) shows the live **open-bug count** and a **polish** bar. There's no preview of the review itself — clean up the bugs and ship when you're happy.

**Reviews**

At launch the product earns a **review score out of 10**, built mainly from its shipped **quality**, plus its design and any **marketing awareness** you built. A higher review means a **bigger launch** (more installed base) and a **larger publisher bonus**. The score appears in the ship report after release.

See [Marketing](siliconalley-marketing), [Product Lifecycle](siliconalley-lifecycle), or the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview).
help_siliconalley-getting-started_conten:
**Getting Started**

**Silicon Alley** lets you run IT companies that design and ship software for a living. This guide takes you from an empty office to your first shipped product.

**1. Found a studio**

Rent or buy an **office** building, then start one of the three Silicon Alley businesses there:

* [Software Engineering Studio](siliconalley-softwarestudio) — business software
* [Cyber Security Firm](siliconalley-cybersecurity) — security audits
* [Game Developer Studio](siliconalley-gamestudio) — video games

**2. Fit out the office**

Your studio needs a little furniture to operate:

* a [Computer Workstation](furniture-computerworkstation) — where your staff do their work (no workstation, no progress)
* [Bathrooms](furniture-bathrooms)

**3. Hire your team**

Staff the studio with **[Programmers](skill-programmer)** hired from **City Workforce Inc.** — other agencies don't offer them. The Software and Game studios also benefit from a **[Graphic Designer](skill-graphicdesigner)**, who leads the Design phase. Seat everyone at a Computer Workstation so they start working.

**4. Design a project**

Open the studio's design window with **F9** and click **Start project**. The [Design Wizard](siliconalley-wizard) walks you through the choices — scope, features, tools, platforms and market. On the Summary, **Start development** locks your plan.

**5. Build, test and ship**

Your team carries the project through four stages, and you push it forward at each one:

* **Design** → **Development** — your programmers build it
* **Development** → **Testing** — *Send to testing* to burn down [bugs](siliconalley-bugs-reviews)
* **Testing** → **Release** — ship whenever you like, but the later you ship the higher the quality

A release pays a one-off launch sum, earns a [review score](siliconalley-bugs-reviews), and grows your installed base.

**6. Keep earning**

After launch the product pays recurring **support income**. Keep it alive with [updates and sequels](siliconalley-lifecycle), boost launches with [marketing](siliconalley-marketing) and [publisher deals](siliconalley-publishers), earn between projects with [contracts](siliconalley-contracts), and time your releases for a [demand peak](siliconalley-market-demand).

**The studio dashboard**

Press **F8** for the studio dashboard — every studio's phase, progress, quality, reputation, installed base, support income and the market-demand trend, at a glance.

**Options**

Tune the mod from the game's options, under **Silicon Alley**:

* **Project type** — the default scope for new projects
* **Project speed**, **Payout multiplier**, **Support income** — pace and difficulty sliders
* **Open project screen** / **Open studio dashboard** — the hotkeys (default **F9** and **F8**)

See the [Silicon Alley overview](siliconalley-overview) for the full guide list.
